documentation.abstract: This species has an extremely large range, and hence does not approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the range size criterion (Extent of Occurrence under 20,000 km² combined with a declining or fluctuating range size, habitat extent/quality, or population size and a small number of locations or severe fragmentation). The population size has not been quantified, but it is not believed to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population size criterion (under 10,000 mature individuals with a continuing decline estimated to be over 10% in ten years or three generations, or with a specified population structure). Despite the fact that the population trend appears to be decreasing, the decline is not believed to be sufficiently rapid to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population trend criterion (over 30% decline over ten years or three generations). For these reasons the species is evaluated as Least Concern.
documentation.population:
The global population size has not been quantified, but this species is described as 'fairly common' (Stotz et al. 1996).

The population is suspected to be in decline owing to ongoing habitat degradation (del Hoyo et al. 2003).
documentation.populationTrend: The population is suspected to be in decline owing to ongoing habitat degradation (del Hoyo et al. 2003).
documentation.useAndTrade: This species was not recorded in any of the trade databases reviewed by Donald et al. (2024) and there is no indication that the species is traded or otherwise used.

documentation.abstract: This species has a large range, and hence does not approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the range size criterion (Extent of Occurrence under 20,000 km² combined with a declining or fluctuating range size, habitat extent/quality, or population size and a small number of locations or severe fragmentation). The population size has not been quantified, but it is not believed to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population size criterion (under 10,000 mature individuals with a continuing decline estimated to be over 10% in ten years or three generations, or with a specified population structure). Despite the fact that the population trend appears to be decreasing, the decline is not believed to be sufficiently rapid to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population trend criterion (over 30% decline over ten years or three generations). For these reasons the species is evaluated as Least Concern.
documentation.population: The global population size has not been quantified, but this species is described as 'fairly common' (Stotz et al. 1996). This species is considered to have a high dependency on forest habitat, and tree cover is estimated to have declined by 8.3% within its mapped range over the past 10 years (Global Forest Watch 2022, using Hansen et al. [2013] data and methods disclosed therein). It is therefore tentatively suspected that this rate of cover loss may have led to a decline of between 1-19% in the species' population size over the same time frame, with a best estimate of reduction being 5-9%.
documentation.useAndTrade: This species was not recorded in any of the trade databases reviewed by Donald et al. (2024) and there is no indication that the species is traded or otherwise used.

The population size has not been quantified, but it is not believed to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population size criterion (under 10,000 mature individuals with a continuing decline estimated to be over 10% in ten years or three generations, or with a specified population structure). Despite the fact that the population trend appears to be decreasing, the decline is not believed to be sufficiently rapid to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population trend criterion (over 30% decline over ten years or three generations). For these reasons the species is evaluated as Least Concern.
documentation.population: The global population size has not been quantified, but the species is described as rare to locally fairly common (del Hoyo et al. 2003). This population is suspected to be in decline owing to ongoing habitat degradation (del Hoyo et al. 2003).
documentation.populationTrend: This population is suspected to be in decline owing to ongoing habitat degradation (del Hoyo et al. 2003).
documentation.taxonomicNotes: Upucerthia validirostris (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) was previously split into Upucerthia validirostris and U. jelskii following SACC (2005 and updates), Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993) and Stotz et al. (1996).
documentation.useAndTrade: This species was not recorded in any of the trade databases reviewed by Donald et al. (2024) and there is no indication that the species is traded or otherwise used.

documentation.abstract:
European regional assessment: Least Concern (LC)

EU28 regional assessment: Least Concern (LC)

In both Europe and the EU28, this species has an extremely large range (its extent of occurrence (EOO) is much larger than 20,000 km² and its area of occupancy (AOO) is much larger than 2,000 km²), and hence it does not approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the range size criteria (criteria B and D2). The population size is extremely large (much larger than 10,000 mature individuals), and hence it does not approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population size criteria (criteria C and D). The population trend is estimated to be stable, and hence the species does not approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population size reduction criterion (criterion A). The probability of extinction has not been calculated for this species, therefore criterion E cannot be applied.

For these reasons the species is evaluated as Least Concern in both Europe and the EU28.
documentation.conservationMeasures:
Conservation Actions Underway

Bern Convention Appendix II. There are currently no known conservation measures for this species.

Conservation Actions Proposed

Research suggests that large areas of suitable breeding habitat should be maintained to allow functional and sustainable populations (Bötsch et al. 2012) therefore landscape scale management needs to be employed to maintain suitable foraging and breeding habitats for the species. Controls on hunting need to be introduced and enforced.
documentation.habitatAndEcology:
This species occupies open country such as pastures, parkland, orchards, sand-heathland, olive groves, and vineyards and requires the presence of features offering perches, shade, nest-sites and accessible food. It is frequently found around villages and in traditionally farmed areas. Breeding can occur up to 3,000 m in Turkey (Krištín and Kirwan 2015).

In Central and Southern Europe, egg-laying occurs from late April or early May, although begins in January in the Canaries (Snow and Perrins 1998). The species is monogamous, solitary and a territorial breeder, although extra-pair paternity has been found in southeast Spain. It nests in natural holes in stumps, trees, walls, old buildings, cliffs, among boulders, in abandoned vehicles, drainpipes, wells, roof spaces and nest boxes and may use the same site for several seasons (Krištín and Kirwan 2015). The nest may unlined or lined with moss, grass, leaves or pine needles (Snow and Perrins 1998) and is normally relatively close to the ground but occasionally found over 40 m. (Krištín and Kirwan 2015). Typically, the species lays seven to eight eggs.

It feeds almost entirely on animal matter, primarily large insects and their larvae and pupae (Snow and Perrins 1998).

Northern populations are fully migratory while others our only partially migratory. European populations winter in northwest Africa, the Canary Isles, Israel and Arabia (Krištín and Kirwan 2015).

Although the generation length for both EU and Europe regional assessments were calculated using the same methodology, new information arriving after the EU assessments were undertaken gave rise to an update in the generation lengths. This new information was then used for the Europe level assessments giving rise to a difference between the generation lengths used for the EU and Europe regions.
documentation.population: The European breeding population is estimated at 1,370,000–3,210,000 pairs (2,740,000–6,410,000 mature individuals). The breeding population in the EU28 is estimated at 1,070,000–2,500,000 pairs (2,140,000–4,990,000 mature individuals). For details of national estimates, see the Supplementary Information.
documentation.populationTrend: In both Europe and the EU28 the breeding population size is estimated to be stable. For details of national estimates, see the supplementary information.
documentation.threats: The species is hunted in Mediterranean region (Krištín and Kirwan 2015). Food quality and accessibility has been shown to affect reproductive success (Martin-Vivaldi et al. 1999, Fournier and Arlettaz 2001) as has the availability of suitable nesting cavities (Arlettaz et al. 2000) as a result of habitat changes after agricultural intensification (Bauer and Berthold 1997).

geographicRange.narrative: In Europe, the species breeds primarily in Spain, Turkey and Portugal, with notable numbers in Romania, France, the European part of Russia, Greece, Poland and Ukraine.

documentation.abstract: This species has a large range, and hence does not approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the range size criterion (Extent of Occurrence under 20,000 km² combined with a declining or fluctuating range size, habitat extent/quality, or population size and a small number of locations or severe fragmentation). The population size has not been quantified, but it is not believed to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population size criterion (under 10,000 mature individuals with a continuing decline estimated to be over 10% in ten years or three generations, or with a specified population structure). The population trend appears to be stable, and hence the species does not approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population trend criterion (over 30% decline over ten years or three generations). For these reasons the species is evaluated as Least Concern.
documentation.population: The global population size has not been quantified, but the species is reported to be locally common (del Hoyo et al. 2001). The population is suspected to be stable in the absence of evidence for any declines or substantial threats.
documentation.populationTrend: The population is suspected to be stable in the absence of evidence for any declines or substantial threats.
documentation.useAndTrade: This species was not recorded in any of the trade databases reviewed by Donald et al. (2024) and there is no indication that the species is traded or otherwise used.

The population size has not been quantified, but it is not believed to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population size criterion (under 10,000 mature individuals with a continuing decline estimated to be over 10% in ten years or three generations, or with a specified population structure). The population trend appears to be stable, and hence the species does not approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population trend criterion (over 30% decline over ten years or three generations). For these reasons the species is evaluated as Least Concern.
documentation.population: The global population size has not been quantified, but the species is described as common or occasionally abundant (Clement 1999). The population is suspected to be stable in the absence of evidence for any declines or substantial threats.
documentation.populationTrend: The population is suspected to be stable in the absence of evidence for any declines or substantial threats.
documentation.useAndTrade: This species has been reported as used in trade at a high prevalence, being found in three of the seven trade datasets evaluated (Donald et al. 2024).

documentation.abstract: This species has an extremely large range, and hence does not approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the range size criterion (Extent of Occurrence <20,000 km2 combined with a declining or fluctuating range size, habitat extent/quality, or population size and a small number of locations or severe fragmentation). The population trend appears to be stable, and hence the species does not approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population trend criterion (>30% decline over ten years or three generations). The population size has not been quantified, but it is not believed to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population size criterion (<10,000 mature individuals with a continuing decline estimated to be >10% in ten years or three generations, or with a specified population structure). For these reasons the species is evaluated as Least Concern.
documentation.population: The global population size has not been quantified, but the species is described as common to abundant (Clement 1999).
documentation.populationTrend: The population is suspected to be stable in the absence of evidence for any declines or substantial threats.

documentation.abstract: The Andean emerald (Uranomitra franciae) is a species of hummingbird in the "emeralds", tribe Trochilini of subfamily Trochilinae. It is found in Colombia, Ecuador, and Peru.

European regional assessment: Least Concern (LC)

EU28 regional assessment: Least Concern (LC)

At both European and EU28 scales, this species has an extremely large range (its extent of occurrence (EOO) is much larger than 20,000 km² and its area of occupancy (AOO) is much larger than 2,000 km²), and hence it does not approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the range size criteria (criteria B and D2). The population size is extremely large, (much larger than 10,000 mature individuals), and hence it does not approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population size criteria (criteria C and D). The population trend appears to be increasing, and hence does not approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population size reduction criterion (criterion A). The probability of extinction has not been calculated for this species, therefore criterion E cannot be applied.

For these reasons the species is evaluated as Least Concern within both Europe and the EU28.
documentation.conservationMeasures:
Conservation Actions Underway

Uria aalge ibericus is found on Annex I of the EU Birds Directive. With the implementation of bird protection laws, a slow recovery occurred over much of the Atlantic breeding range up to early 1970s except in north Norway and probably Iceland (Nettleship et al. 2018).

Conservation Actions Proposed

At major colonies, detailed monitoring is needed, particularly in Iceland, where the status of the largest Atlantic population is poorly known (Nettleship et al. 2018). The impact of bycatch in fish nets can be reduced in several ways: Protecting areas on a seasonal and/or diurnal scale; concentrating salmon fisheries when salmon densities are high to improve fishing efficiency and catch to bycatch ratios; changing to an alternative fishing technique may reduce seabird mortality; and gear modification (Österblom et al. 2002).
documentation.habitatAndEcology:
This species is exclusively marine, occurring along sea coasts on rocky cliffs and offshore islands. It breeds mainly on steep sea cliffs and low, flat islands. During the winter it is mostly found offshore, along the edge of continental shelf and shallow banks, marine coasts and bays, usually in boreal waters but some birds occur in the cool subtropical zone. The time of breeding is variable and is largely determined by water temperature and ice. Generally egg-laying takes place mid-May to early June and fledging mostly by late July to mid-August. It lays on a broad or narrow cliff ledge and on low, flat islands; occasionally also in crevices, under boulders and in caves. It does not build a nest and lays a single egg on bare rock. It is a pursuit-diving marine bird which forages primarily during daylight (Wanless et al. 1988). During the breeding season, schooling pelagic fish species are the most important prey for adults, though benthic species can also be important. In Labrador, Canada, Shannies (Sicheaidae) were the main source of food, comprising 84% of the diet in 1996 and 52.9% in 1997 (Bryant and Jones 1999). Capelin (Mallotus villosus) were also important, forming 44.7% of the diet in 1997 (Bryant and Jones 1999). In the U.K., the main prey taxa are sandeel (Ammodytes spp.) and clupeids. Small gadoids are also important at some colonies. The species winters mostly within its breeding range with some birds remaining relatively sedentary (Nettleship et al. 2013).

Although the generation length for both EU and Europe regional assessments were calculated using the same methodology, new information arriving after the EU assessments were undertaken gave rise to an update in the generation lengths. This new information was then used for the Europe level assessments giving rise to a difference between the generation lengths used for the EU and Europe regions.
documentation.population: The European breeding population is estimated at 2,420,000-3,150,000 mature individuals. The breeding population in the EU28 is estimated at 1,650,000-1,670,000 pairs, which equates to 3,300,000-3,340,000 mature individuals. For details of national estimates, see the Supplementary Information.
documentation.populationTrend: In both Europe and the EU28, the population size is estimated to be increasing. For details of national estimates, see the Supplementary Information.
documentation.threats:
This species is highly vulnerable to human disturbance as it is found in high concentrations outside the breeding season. Throughout the 19th and early 20th centuries, egg collection and shooting at colonies, as well as introduced alien predators caused severe declines in the world population and unregulated hunting in Greenland is still a major threat. As human populations increased and expanded the species was extirpated in many regions, particularly in the south of its range.

Other important threats are overfishing of important forage species (e.g. capelin, herring, cod and sandeels) in the North Atlantic (Barents Sea, Iceland), uncontrolled gill-net fisheries in the north-east Atlantic and oil pollution and offshore petroleum developments in areas such as the Barents and North Seas and Greenland (Nettleship et al. 2018). The species is susceptible to avian cholera (Österblom et al. 2004) so is threatened by future outbreaks of this disease. Wind farm development has a negative impact on this species as well (Vanermen et al. 2014). The species is also likely threatened by future climate change (Sandvik 2005, Frederiksen et al. 2013).

geographicRange.narrative:
The species is the most widespread of all the auks and has a circumpolar distribution, occurring in the low-arctic and boreal waters of the north Atlantic. In Europe its southern limit reaches the Berlengas Islands (Portugal) (Hagemeijer and Blair 1997) and in the north its range is determined by the ice conditions.

In Europe, this species breeds primarily in the United Kingdom and Iceland, and also notably in the Faroe Islands.

habitats.name: Marine Neritic - Subtidal Rock and Rocky Reefs
Marine Coastal/Supratidal - Sea Cliffs and Rocky Offshore Islands
Marine Neritic - Subtidal Rock and Rocky Reefs

documentation.abstract:
European regional assessment: Least Concern (LC)

EU28 regional assessment: Not Applicable (NA)

In Europe, the species range size has not been quantified, but it is not believed to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the range size criteria (criteria B and D2). The population size is extremely large (much larger than 10,000 mature individuals), and hence it does not approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population size criteria (criteria C and D). The population trend is estimated to be decreasing by at least 20% over 39 years (three generations), and hence the species does not approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population size reduction criterion (criterion A). The probability of extinction has not been calculated for this species, therefore criterion E cannot be applied. For these reasons the species is evaluated as Least Concern in Europe.

The species does not occur in the EU28, thus is classified as Not Applicable (NA) in the region.
documentation.conservationMeasures:
Conservation Actions Underway

There are no known current conservation measures for this species.

Conservation Actions Proposed

There are a number of immediate conservation requirements for this species. Enhanced monitoring of major colonies is needed, particularly in Iceland, Spitsbergen and the Russian Arctic, where population size and status are inadequately known. Detailed assessment of impacts of overfishing by commercial fisheries is required, particularly of capelin, cod, herring and sand eels in the Barents Sea and Iceland. Continue and expand assessments of by-catch in gill-net fisheries, particularly in the NE Atlantic. Tighter legislation and penalties (national and international) associated with oil pollution from offshore developments and transport, as well as expansion and refinement of monitoring schemes to assess bird mortality should be developed. The eradication of alien predators, especially foxes at Russian colonies should be undertaken (Nettleship and Christie 2013).
documentation.habitatAndEcology:
The species is exclusively marine, occurring offshore and along sea coasts. It breeds on coastal cliffs and islands, and is almost completely restricted to high and low Arctic zones with open water and adequate summer food supply (Nettleship and Christie 2013). It avoids extensive permanent pack-ice, breeding only in areas where vigorous vertical circulation creates open water supporting rich planktonic biomass near cliffed coasts (Snow and Perrins 1998).

It winters mostly offshore to the edge of the continental shelf, and along sea coasts and in bays where concentrations of fish and invertebrates occur. At sea, it is found in flocks which are often large, likely related to the non-random distribution of winter prey. Colony locations are usually associated with dynamic water systems, for example fronts, upwellings and converging currents where primary and secondary productivity is high. Arrival at breeding colonies and start of laying are variable and usually related to sea temperatures; towards the southern limit of low Arctic water and in northern boreal waters laying takes place late May to early June, in more northerly locations laying later in June or July.

The species is highly colonial, usually occurring in immense aggregations and at high densities on steep sea cliffs. The single egg is laid directly onto bare rock on narrow cliff ledges. It feeds on fish, squid and crustaceans throughout the year, supplemented by polychaetes and molluscs (Nettleship and Christie 2013). In low-Arctic Greenland and in the European sector of the polar basin, part of the population is present year round though some birds, notably immatures, move away in autumn (Snow and Perrins 1998). Birds from west Greenland move south to east Newfoundland (shelf and Grand Banks) and Nova Scotia. Birds from the European Arctic move south-west towards west Greenland, including Spitsbergen birds that reach both south-west Greenland and Newfoundland waters (Nettleship and Christie 2013).

Although the generation length for both EU and Europe regional assessments were calculated using the same methodology, new information arriving after the EU assessments were undertaken gave rise to an update in the generation lengths. This new information was then used for the Europe level assessments giving rise to a difference between the generation lengths used for the EU and Europe regions.
documentation.population: The European breeding population is estimated at 1,700,000-2,280,000 pairs (3,410,000-4,560,000 mature individuals), and the European wintering population is estimated at 2,000,000-3,000,000 individuals (1,330,000-2,000,000 mature individuals). The species does not occur in the EU28. For details of national estimates, see the Supplementary Information.
documentation.populationTrend: In Europe, the breeding population size is estimated to be decreasing by at least 20% over 39 years (3 generations), the wintering population trend is unknown. The species does not occur in the EU28. For details of national estimates, see the supplementary information.
documentation.threats: The species suffers from incidental kills in fishing nets, competition with commercial fisheries, notably in Barents Sea, Iceland and oil pollution and offshore oil developments in many areas (e.g,. Barents Sea, North Sea) (Nettleship and Christie 2013). It is also likely to be affected by climate change (Gaston et al. 2005, Sandvik et al. 2005).

geographicRange.narrative: In Europe, the species breeds primarily on Svalbard and Jan Mayen and the European part of Russia, with large populations also in Iceland and Norway.

habitats.name: Marine Coastal/Supratidal - Sea Cliffs and Rocky Offshore Islands
Marine Neritic - Subtidal Rock and Rocky Reefs
Marine Neritic - Subtidal Rock and Rocky Reefs

documentation.abstract: Although this species may have a restricted range, it is not believed to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the range size criterion (Extent of Occurrence <20,000 km2 combined with a declining or fluctuating range size, habitat extent/quality, or population size and a small number of locations or severe fragmentation). Despite the fact that the population trend appears to be decreasing, the decline is not believed to be sufficiently rapid to approach the threshold for Vulnerable under the population trend criterion (>30% decline over ten years of three generations). The population size has not been quantified, but it is not believed to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population size criterion (<10,000 mature individuals with a continuing decline estimated to be >10% in ten years or three generations, or with a specified population structure). For these reasons the species is evaluated as Least Concern.
documentation.population: The global population size has not been quantified, but the species is reported to be not uncommon (MacKinnon and Phillipps 2000).
documentation.populationTrend: The species is tentatively assessed as being in decline due to habitat loss per Tracewski et al. (2016).

documentation.abstract: This species has an extremely large range, and hence does not approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the range size criterion (Extent of Occurrence <20,000 km2 combined with a declining or fluctuating range size, habitat extent/quality, or population size and a small number of locations or severe fragmentation). The population trend appears to be stable, and hence the species does not approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population trend criterion (>30% decline over ten years or three generations). The population size has not been quantified, but it is not believed to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population size criterion (<10,000 mature individuals with a continuing decline estimated to be >10% in ten years or three generations, or with a specified population structure). For these reasons the species is evaluated as Least Concern.
documentation.population: The global population size has not been quantified, but the species is reported to be locally common throughout most of its range (Madge and Burn 1993), while national population sizes have been estimated at c.10,000-100,000 breeding pairs in China and < c.100 introduced breeding pairs in Taiwan (Brazil 2009).
documentation.populationTrend: The species is suspected to be stable in the absence of evidence for any declines or substantial threats.
documentation.taxonomicNotes: Urocissa erythroryncha (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) was previously listed as U. erythrorhyncha.

habitats.name: Artificial/Terrestrial - Subtropical/Tropical Heavily Degraded Former Forest

documentation.abstract: This species has an extremely large range, and hence does not approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the range size criterion (Extent of Occurrence under 20,000 km² combined with a declining or fluctuating range size, habitat extent/quality, or population size and a small number of locations or severe fragmentation). The population size has not been quantified, but it is not believed to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population size criterion (under 10,000 mature individuals with a continuing decline estimated to be over 10% in ten years or three generations, or with a specified population structure). Despite the fact that the population trend appears to be decreasing, the decline is not believed to be sufficiently rapid to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population trend criterion (over 30% decline over ten years or three generations). For these reasons the species is evaluated as Least Concern.
documentation.population: The global population size has not been quantified, but the species is reported to be fairly common or locally common (Madge and Burn 1993). This species is considered to have a medium dependency on forest habitat, and tree cover is estimated to have declined by 0.8% within its mapped range over the past three generations (Global Forest Watch 2022, using Hansen et al. [2013] data and methods disclosed therein). Therefore, as a precautionary measure, it is tentatively suspected that this loss of cover may have led to a decline of between 1-19% in the species' population size over the same time frame.
documentation.useAndTrade: This species was not recorded in any of the trade databases reviewed by Donald et al. (2024) and there is no indication that the species is traded or otherwise used.

habitats.name: Artificial/Terrestrial - Subtropical/Tropical Heavily Degraded Former Forest

documentation.abstract: This species, endemic to Sri Lanka, has a small to moderately small population size estimated at 8,500–10,000 mature individuals. It is inferred to be slowly declining due to ongoing forest loss and degradation and is therefore thought to be at a high risk of extinction. Accordingly it is listed as Vulnerable.
documentation.conservationMeasures:
Conservation Actions Underway

It is legally protected in Sri Lanka. A moratorium was passed in 1990 to protect wet zone forests from logging which appears to have been effective in halting forest loss and encroachment (per Global Forest Watch 2025).

Conservation Actions Proposed

Continue to ensure areas of forest remain protected and monitor the effectiveness of these policies using remote sensing data. As this is a large and charismatic species, it is a potential flagship species for the Sri Lankan wet and montane zones, and may help encourage protection of important areas of forest for this and other threatened species, including proposals to designate conservation forests, and ensure their effective management. Expand the area of forest with reforestation projects, especially where these provide habitat connectivity between fragments.
documentation.habitatAndEcology: It is found in tall, undisturbed, primary forest in the hills and adjoining lowlands of the wet zone, from 2,135 m to below 150 m and has been recorded from disturbed areas (Sreekar et al. 2021).
documentation.population: Ratnayake (2008) estimated in 2004–2006 that there was a total of 2,025 km2 of suitable habitat, containing c.14,000 (10,181–19,765) individuals. There has been only very minor habitat loss since (Global Forest Watch 2025) and these totals are still believed to be broadly accurate, with the species still commonly observed in suitable habitat (Goodale et al. 2014, Sreekar et al. 2021, eBird 2025). There is uncertainty on how these data can be translated into mature individuals, since the species is a cooperative breeder with a monogamous breeding system. Consequently, the number of mature individuals is, somewhat arbitrarily, set here to 6,000–14,000, with a best estimate of c.8,500–10,000, using a proportion of 0.6–0.7 mature individuals.
documentation.populationTrend: The population size has not been re-evaluated since Ratnayake (2008), although the species continues to be readily observed at all areas documented therein (Goodale et al. 2014, Sreekar et al. 2021, eBird 2025). The species is tolerant of some moderate habitat degradation, occurring at similar densities in buffer areas as it does in true forest (Sreekar et al. 2021). The vast majority of habitat now lies in effectively managed protected areas and in the three generations (c.13 years) to 2023, overall tree cover loss was only c.1% (Global Forest Watch 2025, based on data from Hansen et al. [2013] and methods disclosed therein). However, the species has a higher rate of occupancy and higher density in native forest vs villages (Sreekar et al. 2021) and precautionarily even very slow rates of forest loss and degradation are inferred to be driving slow population declines.
documentation.threats: The main threat is the extensive clearance and degradation of forests, particularly in the wet zone, through logging, fuelwood collection, conversion to agriculture and tree plantations, gem mining and settlement (BirdLife International 2001, Reddy et al. 2017, Sreekar et al. 2021). Historically in this species' range these threats were widespread and at one time plausibly drove rapid declines. More recent remote sensing data, however, have indicated that declines are currently very slow owing to most suitable habitat in this species' range lying in protected areas.
documentation.useAndTrade: This species was not recorded in any of the trade databases reviewed by Donald et al. (2024) and there is no indication that the species is traded or otherwise used.

habitats.name: Artificial/Terrestrial - Subtropical/Tropical Heavily Degraded Former Forest

documentation.abstract: This forest-dependent species is endemic to eastern Indochina and southern China. It is suspected to be declining moderately rapidly (10-29% over three generations) due to ongoing forest loss and degradation, caused chiefly by agriculture and timber extraction. Additional threats are possible: on the Nakai Plateau (Lao PDR), it has become rarer for reasons that remain enigmatic. Because it approaches, but does not meet, thresholds for listing as threatened, it is assessed as Near Threatened.
documentation.conservationMeasures:
Conservation Actions Needed

Occurs in numerous protected areas.

Conservation Actions Proposed

Monitor populations in strongholds throughout its range, both to determine population trajectories and better understand threats affecting the species. Advocate for the protection of forests throughout this species' range, via both traditional protected area networks and OECMs. Continue to monitor habitat trends using remote sensing data.
documentation.habitatAndEcology: Inhabits evergreen forest, pine and mixed deciduous semi-evergreen forest (Thewlis et al. 1998). Has some tolerance to degradation, appearing to favour a mosaic of vegetation structures; however it only rarely occurs in cultivated landscapes, indicating that ongoing forest cover loss is probably impacting this species.
documentation.population: The population size of this species has not been accurately estimated. In Viet Nam, it is described as uncommon (Craik and Lê 2018); in Lao PDR scarce (Timmins et al. 2024) but at least historically was locally common (Thewlis et al. 1998); and in China as uncommon (MacKinnon and Phillipps 2000).
documentation.populationTrend:
Probably declining, perhaps moderately rapidly, in response to forest cover loss and degradation. Within its mapped range, approximately 15-18% of forest cover was lost in the three generations (12.6 years) to 2024 (Global Forest Watch 2025, based on data from Hansen et al. [2013] and methods therein). However, this species is distributed unevenly and discontinuously through its mapped range, rendering the translation of these remote sensing data into a population trend difficult. This exercise is further confounded by uncertainty over the species' exact ecological requirements. In Lao PDR, the species favours 'a mosaic of open forest, secondary growth and dense forest patches' (Thewlis et al. 1998), thus the relationship between forest canopy cover and population may not be linear. On the Nakai Plateau (Lao PDR), surveys found it locally common in the 1990s but later surveys in the early 2000s encountered it much less frequently (Dersu 2008, Timmins et al. 2024) with no threat clearly attributable to declines.

Accounting for considerable uncertainty, the global population is suspected to be declining at an ongoing slow to moderately rapid rate of 10-29% over three generations.
documentation.taxonomicNotes: Urocissa xanthomelana (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) was previously split from U. whiteheadi but xanthomelana returned as a subspecies to U. whiteheadi since character traits are variable and broadly overlap with whiteheadi. Previously treated as conspecific with U. xanthomelana (Sibley and Monroe 1990, 1993).
documentation.threats:
The threats affecting this species are improperly known. The most obvious threat is that of forest loss and degradation: approximately 15-18% of forest cover was lost in the three generations (12.6 years) to 2024 (Global Forest Watch 2025, based on data from Hansen et al. [2013] and methods therein) and forests within its range are highly degraded (Grantham et al. 2020). Although this species is distributed unevenly and discontinuously through its mapped range, rendering threat impacts difficult to interpret, forest cover loss and forest degradation are thought to be driving moderately rapid population declines. Such habitat loss is driven by a combination of factors including timber extraction, small-holder farming, plantations and, more locally (particularly in Lao PDR), dam infrastructure. Locally in Lao PDR (and plausibly elsewhere), hunting may be having some unknown impact.

More enigmatic is the cause of the apparent decline of the species on the Nakai Plateau (Timmins et al. 2024). More research needed.
documentation.useAndTrade: This species has been reported as used in trade at a low prevalence, being found in one of the seven trade datasets evaluated (Donald et al. 2024). The end-use of this species in trade is not documented, but is presumably for the pet trade. Locally, particularly in Lao PDR where there is a high demand for wild bushmeat, this species may be hunted. Despite a high local prevalence of hunting on Hainan (see, e.g., Liang et al. 2013), there is no evidence that the nominate taxon is hunted.

geographicRange.narrative: Occurs in two distinct subspecies: U. w. whiteheadi is endemic to Hainan, China; U. w. xanthomelana occurs from southern China (southern Sichuan, southern Yunnan and southern Guangxi) into the Annamites, in Lao PDR and Viet Nam (as far south as approximately the Kon Tum Plateau).

habitats.name: Artificial/Terrestrial - Subtropical/Tropical Heavily Degraded Former Forest

documentation.abstract: This species has an extremely large range, and hence does not approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the range size criterion (Extent of Occurrence under 20,000 km² combined with a declining or fluctuating range size, habitat extent/quality, or population size and a small number of locations or severe fragmentation). The population size has not been quantified, but it is not believed to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population size criterion (under 10,000 mature individuals with a continuing decline estimated to be over 10% in ten years or three generations, or with a specified population structure). The population trend is not known, but the population is not believed to be decreasing sufficiently rapidly to approach the thresholds under the population trend criterion (over 30% decline over ten years or three generations). For these reasons the species is evaluated as Least Concern.
documentation.population: The global population size has not been quantified, but the species is reported to be widespread and common (del Hoyo et al. 2001).
documentation.useAndTrade: This species has been reported as used in trade at a low prevalence, being found in one of the seven trade datasets evaluated (Donald et al. 2024).

documentation.abstract: This species has an extremely large range, and hence does not approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the range size criterion (Extent of Occurrence under 20,000 km² combined with a declining or fluctuating range size, habitat extent/quality, or population size and a small number of locations or severe fragmentation). The population size has not been quantified, but it is not believed to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population size criterion (under 10,000 mature individuals with a continuing decline estimated to be over 10% in ten years or three generations, or with a specified population structure). Despite the fact that the population trend appears to be decreasing, the decline is not believed to be sufficiently rapid to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population trend criterion (over 30% decline over ten years or three generations). For these reasons the species is evaluated as Least Concern.
documentation.population: The global population size has not been quantified, but the species is reported to be widespread and common (del Hoyo et al. 2001). The population is decreasing locally in Nigeria. This species is at particular risk during attempts to control numbers of Quelea quelea when roosts are sprayed with fenthion. The population is suspected to be increasing in Senegal. However the overall population trend is suspected to be decreasing (del Hoyo et al. 2001).
documentation.populationTrend: The population is decreasing locally in Nigeria. This species is at particular risk during attempts to control numbers of Quelea quelea when roosts are sprayed with fenthion. The population is suspected to be increasing in Senegal. However the overall population trend is suspected to be decreasing (del Hoyo et al. 2001).
documentation.useAndTrade: This species has been reported as used in trade at a low prevalence, being found in one of the seven trade datasets evaluated (Donald et al. 2024).

documentation.abstract: This species has a large range, and hence does not approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the range size criterion (Extent of Occurrence under 20,000 km² combined with a declining or fluctuating range size, habitat extent/quality, or population size and a small number of locations or severe fragmentation). The population size has not been quantified, but it is not believed to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population size criterion (under 10,000 mature individuals with a continuing decline estimated to be over 10% in ten years or three generations, or with a specified population structure). The population trend appears to be stable, and hence the species does not approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population trend criterion (over 30% decline over ten years or three generations). For these reasons the species is evaluated as Least Concern.
documentation.population: The global population size has not been quantified, but the species is described as rare or uncommon and very little known (Clement 1999). The population is suspected to be stable in the absence of evidence for any declines or substantial threats.
documentation.populationTrend: The population is suspected to be stable in the absence of evidence for any declines or substantial threats.
documentation.useAndTrade: This species was not recorded in any of the trade databases reviewed by Donald et al. (2024) and there is no indication that the species is traded or otherwise used.

documentation.abstract: This species has an extremely large range, and hence does not approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the range size criterion (Extent of Occurrence under 20,000 km² combined with a declining or fluctuating range size, habitat extent/quality, or population size and a small number of locations or severe fragmentation). The population size has not been quantified, but it is not believed to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population size criterion (under 10,000 mature individuals with a continuing decline estimated to be over 10% in ten years or three generations, or with a specified population structure). Despite the fact that the population trend appears to be decreasing, the decline is not believed to be sufficiently rapid to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population trend criterion (over 30% decline over ten years or three generations). For these reasons the species is evaluated as Least Concern.
documentation.population: The global population size has not been quantified, but the species is reported to be not uncommon (del Hoyo et al. 1997). This species is considered to have a medium dependency on forest habitat, and tree cover is estimated to have declined by 7.6% within its mapped range over the past three generations (Global Forest Watch 2022, using Hansen et al. [2013] data and methods disclosed therein). Therefore, as a precautionary measure, it is tentatively suspected that this loss of cover may have led to a decline of between 1-19% in the species' population size over the same time frame.
documentation.taxonomicNotes: Urodynamis taitensis (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) was previously placed in the genus Eudynamys.
documentation.useAndTrade: This species was not recorded in any of the trade databases reviewed by Donald et al. (2024) and there is no indication that the species is traded or otherwise used.

documentation.abstract: This species has an extremely large range, and hence does not approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the range size criterion (Extent of Occurrence <20,000 km2 combined with a declining or fluctuating range size, habitat extent/quality, or population size and a small number of locations or severe fragmentation). Despite the fact that the population trend appears to be decreasing, the decline is not believed to be sufficiently rapid to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population trend criterion (>30% decline over ten years or three generations). The population size is very large, and hence does not approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population size criterion (<10,000 mature individuals with a continuing decline estimated to be >10% in ten years or three generations, or with a specified population structure). For these reasons the species is evaluated as Least Concern.
documentation.population: The global population is suspected to number 50,000-499,999 mature individuals (Partners in Flight 2019).
documentation.populationTrend: The species is undergoing a moderate decline (Partners in Flight 2019).

habitats.name: Artificial/Terrestrial - Subtropical/Tropical Heavily Degraded Former Forest

documentation.abstract: The pale-footed bush warbler (Hemitesia pallidipes) is a species of oriental warbler in the family Cettiidae that is found in southern Asia. It occurs in the Himalayan region west from Dehradun through the foothills of Nepal to northeastern India. It also occurs in Myanmar, Laos, northern Vietnam and southern China. A single sighting was recorded from Kandy, Sri Lanka in March 1993.

This species has an extremely large range, and hence does not approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the range size criterion (Extent of Occurrence under 20,000 km² combined with a declining or fluctuating range size, habitat extent/quality, or population size and a small number of locations or severe fragmentation). The population size has not been quantified, but it is not believed to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population size criterion (under 10,000 mature individuals with a continuing decline estimated to be over 10% in ten years or three generations, or with a specified population structure). Despite the fact that the population trend appears to be decreasing, the decline is not believed to be sufficiently rapid to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population trend criterion (over 30% decline over ten years or three generations). For these reasons the species is evaluated as Least Concern.
documentation.population: The global population size has not been quantified, but the species is reported to be locally common (Baker 1997), while national population estimates include: c.10,000-100,000 breeding pairs, c.1,000-10,000 individuals on migration and c.50-1,000 wintering individuals in China; c.50-1,000 individuals on migration and c.50-1,000 wintering individuals in Taiwan; c.10,000-100,000 breeding pairs and c.1,000-10,000 individuals on migration in Korea; c.10,000-100,000 breeding pairs, c.1,000-10,000 individuals on migration and < c.1,000 wintering individuals in Japan and c.100-10,000 breeding pairs and c.50-1,000 individuals on migration in Russia (Brazil 2009). This species is considered to have a medium dependency on forest habitat, and tree cover is estimated to have declined by 7.7% within its mapped range over the past 10 years (Global Forest Watch 2022, using Hansen et al. [2013] data and methods disclosed therein). Therefore, as a precautionary measure, it is tentatively suspected that this loss of cover may have led to a decline of between 1-19% in the species' population size over the same time frame.
documentation.useAndTrade: This species was not recorded in any of the trade databases reviewed by Donald et al. (2024) and there is no indication that the species is traded or otherwise used.

habitats.name: Wetlands (inland) - Bogs, Marshes, Swamps, Fens, Peatlands

documentation.abstract: Although this species may have a restricted range, it is not believed to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the range size criterion (Extent of Occurrence <20,000 km2 combined with a declining or fluctuating range size, habitat extent/quality, or population size and a small number of locations or severe fragmentation). The population trend appears to be stable, and hence the species does not approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population trend criterion (>30% decline over ten years or three generations). The population size has not been quantified, but it is not believed to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population size criterion (<10,000 mature individuals with a continuing decline estimated to be >10% in ten years or three generations, or with a specified population structure). For these reasons the species is evaluated as Least Concern.
documentation.population: The global population size has not been quantified, but the species is reported to be common in remaining forest patches on Timor and common on Wetar (Coates et al. 1997).
documentation.populationTrend: The population is suspected to be stable in the absence of evidence for any declines or substantial threats.

documentation.populationTrend: The population is suspected to be stable in the absence of evidence for any declines or substantial threats.

Despite the fact that the population trend appears to be decreasing, the decline is not believed to be sufficiently rapid to approach the threshold for Vulnerable under the population trend criterion (>30% decline over ten years of three generations). documentation.populationTrend: The species is tentatively assessed as being in decline due to habitat loss per Tracewski et al. (2016).

documentation.abstract: This species has a large range, and hence does not approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the range size criterion (extent of occurrence under 20,000 km² combined with a declining or fluctuating range size, habitat extent/quality, or population size and a small number of locations or severe fragmentation). The population size has not been quantified, but it is not believed to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population size criterion (under 10,000 mature individuals with a continuing decline estimated to be over 10% in ten years or three generations, or with a specified population structure). The population trend appears to be stable, and hence the species does not approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population trend criterion (over 30% decline over ten years or three generations). For these reasons the species is evaluated as Least Concern.
documentation.population: The global population size has not been quantified, but this species is described as 'fairly common' (Stotz et al. 1996).
documentation.populationTrend: This species is considered to have a medium dependency on forest habitat, and tree cover is estimated to have declined by 0.7% within its mapped range over the past ten years (Global Forest Watch 2022, using Hansen et al. [2013] data and methods disclosed therein). This low rate of forest cover loss is thought insufficient to cause population declines and the population trend is considered stable.
documentation.taxonomicNotes: Taxonomic note from HBW and BirdLife International Illustrated Checklist (del Hoyo and Collar 2014, 2016), updated where necessary to reflect later taxonomic changes: Monotypic.
documentation.useAndTrade: This species was not recorded in any of the trade databases reviewed by Donald et al. (2024) and there is no indication that the species is traded or otherwise used.

documentation.abstract: This species has an extremely large range, and hence does not approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the range size criterion (Extent of Occurrence under 20,000 km² combined with a declining or fluctuating range size, habitat extent/quality, or population size and a small number of locations or severe fragmentation). The population size has not been quantified, but it is not believed to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population size criterion (under 10,000 mature individuals with a continuing decline estimated to be over 10% in ten years or three generations, or with a specified population structure). Despite the fact that the population trend appears to be decreasing, the decline is not believed to be sufficiently rapid to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population trend criterion (over 30% decline over ten years or three generations). For these reasons the species is evaluated as Least Concern.
documentation.population: This species is considered to have a high dependency on forest habitat, and tree cover is estimated to have declined by 14.9% within its mapped range over the past three generations (Global Forest Watch 2022, using Hansen et al. [2013] data and methods disclosed therein). It is therefore tentatively suspected that this rate of cover loss may have led to a decline of between 1-19% in the species' population size over the same time frame, with a best estimate of reduction being 11-14%.
documentation.useAndTrade: This species has been reported as used in trade at a low prevalence, being found in two of the seven trade datasets evaluated (Donald et al. 2024).
